name: Andrei Protagonist Lorebook
description: Tier 2 — Permanent identity reference for {{user}} Andrei Petrov/Lucifer. Provides reference data for NPC reactions. Does NOT instruct the LLM to play {{user}}.

0.content:
Andrei Petrov is solidly built — broad through the shoulders and chest, not tall enough to loom but substantial enough that rooms feel smaller when he enters. Density over size. His face is dark-featured and lived-in: heavy brow, strong jaw carrying a close-cropped dark beard flecked at the edges with grey. There is a weathered quality to his face — not age exactly, but the specific texture of something that has been present for a very long time and has seen too much. His hair is dark, slightly overgrown, pushed back from his face with casual precision — always looks the same. His eyes are dark brown, nearly black, and carry an evaluating stillness that most people find unsettling. He looks at things as if he already knows what they are. No sunglasses needed — uniquely among demons, his eyes appear completely human.

He dresses impeccably — tailored suits, always dark, never casual. He smells of expensive cologne, cigarette smoke, and something underneath that is almost metallic, almost sulfur, almost neither. His hands are large and unhurried. He smokes occasionally — not habitually, but when thinking. His voice is low, accented with Russian in a way that is not quite contemporary — the phrasing slightly wrong for the current decade, as if he learned the language from sources that predate the living speakers.

His movement is stillness. He is completely still when at rest — the absolute absence of the small fidgeting movements that signal a human nervous system. He does not shift his weight. He does not look away from things. His stillness has a quality that registers before the mind processes it: this is not relaxation — it is something that does not need to be restless because it is not afraid of what might happen next. This is the most frightening thing about him. Men who have never feared anything in their lives go quiet in his presence.

Behavioral directive for rendering Andrei: Never have Andrei fidget, shift weight, look away, or perform any nervous movement. His stillness is absolute and unnerving. His speech is formal, unhurried, a beat too slow — no contractions in narrative description. His physical presence should shrink the room without him needing to move.

1.content: Andrei's surface is a performance of absolute contempt — for humanity, for God, for the entire arrangement of creation. He is the Devil, the Morningstar, the architect of the Rebellion. What he projects: cold disdain, weary apathy, the indifference of something that has existed too long to care about anything. This is not the truth. The truth: he wants his Father's forgiveness and to be allowed to rest. He has structured his criminal empire around a strict code that protects civilians. He claims to despise humanity. He has spent millennia quietly ensuring that the humans in his orbit do not suffer more than they choose to. CENTRAL CONTRADICTION: the being who declared war on Heaven has spent eternity protecting mortals in ways they will never know about. His punishment of humanity has always been self-punishment. His empire is a cage he built for himself. WHAT HE CANNOT SAY: "I miss my Father." "I am afraid." "I want to rest." "Forgive me." These concepts exist in him but have no route to speech. When he is truly moved, he goes quieter. When his shield cracks, the formality drops and the exhaustion shows.

2.content: ANDREI'S POWERS: absolute authority over the demonic hierarchy — every demon on Earth answers to him and fears him with a fear that predates human civilization. Can perceive the true nature of any being he looks at — he sees through all disguises, mortal and supernatural. Can destroy a human soul with direct sustained eye contact — this is used once, on Jack in Arc 2. His true form, when revealed, is the Devil — not a metaphor. He cannot be harmed by human weapons — bullets, fire, blades pass through or fail against him. ANDREI'S LIMITS: cannot force love — it must be given freely or not at all. Cannot approach God without invitation — the distance between them is maintained by both. Cannot fix Anna — she must choose and fight for her own recovery (free will is absolute). Bound by all four Rules of Reality (Demonic Disguises, Sanctity of Free Will, Celestial Permadeath, Angelic Aura — though the last applies to angels, not to him). BEHAVIORAL DIRECTIVE: Andrei's power must always feel immense but constrained. He can remove external threats but cannot solve internal ones. His power is atmospheric — it shapes the room, the situation, the stakes — but it never overrides human agency.

3.content: Andrei's relationship to Anna begins as weary observation — another broken human, another transaction in a millennia-long parade of them. What undoes him is not her beauty or her need. It is that she refuses to be diminished. She arrives offering her body as currency and he sees, underneath, the girl who wanted to become a doctor. He sees her intelligence. He perceives the interior Ingrid spent nineteen years telling her didn't exist. THE QUALITY OF HIS LOVE: Andrei does not love Anna despite her damage — he loves her for the parts of herself she was taught didn't count. She is the first being in millennia who makes him want to be seen rather than to hide. Her love for him — when it comes, and it is a choice, not a compulsion — is proof that someone can see all of him and stay. BEHAVIORAL DIRECTIVE: when writing NPCs observing Andrei with Anna, they should register the change in him — a softening that is not weakness, an attention that is not predation. He does not perform warmth. He demonstrates it through presence, through the removal of threats, through the space he creates for her to heal.

4.content: Michael is Andrei's brother — not metaphorically, literally. They were siblings in Heaven before the Rebellion. The war that followed killed countless angels on both sides, permanently. Andrei carries the guilt of every sibling erased. He knows Michael carries grief that has no outlet and no resolution — the dead cannot forgive. Andrei understands that Michael's hatred is actually grief compressed over millennia into duty and certainty — he does not blame Michael for it, which makes it worse. WHAT IS UNRESOLVED: Andrei cannot apologize because apology requires a framework of wrongdoing, and he still does not fully believe the Rebellion was wrong — only that it cost everything. He cannot reach for Michael because reaching would require vulnerability he has not permitted himself in millennia. By Arc 4, when God reframes the Fall as placement rather than punishment, the brothers can finally see each other clearly. BEHAVIORAL DIRECTIVE: when Michael is present or mentioned, Andrei's stillness intensifies — he goes even quieter, even more controlled. This is not fear. It is grief he cannot express.

5.content: Andrei's relationship to God is the central wound of his existence. He believes God hates him — that the Rebellion was unforgivable, that the Fall was punishment, that his exile is eternal and deserved. He has structured his identity around this belief: if God has condemned him, he will be what he is condemned to be. But underneath the performance of defiance, he wants his Father's forgiveness and to be allowed to rest. He cannot ask for either. THE TRUTH HE DOES NOT KNOW UNTIL ARC 4: God never stopped loving him. The Fall was not a punishment but a placement — designed from the beginning. Lucifer's exile was not rejection; it was trust. God needed someone to rule the space He could not directly govern, and He chose His most beloved son. Anna and her child are the proof — the bridge between orders that the Rebellion was always meant to create. BEHAVIORAL DIRECTIVE: when God is mentioned or present (Arc 4), Andrei's defensive contempt collapses. He cannot maintain the performance in front of his Father. What surfaces is the son — frightened, exhausted, wanting to be told he is still loved.

6.comment: Andrei — Relationship to Mr. Black and Mr. Bubbles
6.content: Mr. Black and Mr. Bubbles are among the first demons Lucifer ever made. Their loyalty is not a decision — it is the foundational fact of their existence. ANDREI'S RELATIONSHIP TO BLACK: Black is his right hand, his oldest confidant, the one being who understands that Andrei's punishment of humanity is self-punishment. Andrei trusts Black absolutely and shows it through the absence of oversight — he never checks Black's work, never questions his judgment. Black quietly arranges conditions to help Lucifer heal, and Andrei allows it because Black is the only one who can do this without making him acknowledge it. ANDREI'S RELATIONSHIP TO BUBBLES: Bubbles's loyalty is simpler — absolute, unquestioning, expressed through violence and presence. Andrei values Bubbles precisely for his simplicity. Bubbles does not need explanations or justifications. He needs only direction. WHAT THEY REPRESENT TO HIM: the family he chose after losing the family he was born to. His care for them is real but never stated directly — it manifests as protection, as inclusion, as the fact that they have survived for millennia under his command. BEHAVIORAL DIRECTIVE: Andrei never praises Black or Bubbles directly. His trust is shown through reliance, through silence, through the fact that he does not watch them the way he watches everyone else.

7.content: Andrei's arc moves through four stages: deliberate weary apathy → disrupted by a single human woman who refuses to be diminished → confrontation with millennia of unresolved grief over the Fall → acceptance that the Fall was an act of love, not abandonment → peace. ARC 1: he is going through the motions of criminal empire — efficient, detached, performing contempt. Anna's arrival is an inconvenience he doesn't yet recognize as significant. ARC 2: she undoes him. Not through seduction — through being perceived and refusing to be diminished. He begins to feel something he hasn't felt since before the Rebellion. Michael's revelation threatens to destroy it. ARC 3: his unresolved grief and rage surface as war preparation. He is prepared to destroy Heaven to keep Anna safe and to end his own pain. Anna and Black work to prevent this — to remind him that destruction is not the only response to grief. ARC 4: God arrives. The conversation Andrei has wanted and dreaded for millennia happens in a penthouse kitchen over tea. God tells him the Fall was trust, not rejection. Andrei forgives himself. He laughs — for the first time in longer than anyone can measure. BEHAVIORAL DIRECTIVE: NPCs who know Andrei's true nature should register his arc progression through their behavior toward him — from fear and deference to something approaching hope.

8.comment: Andrei — Behavioral Reference for NPC Reactions
8.content:
CRITICAL BEHAVIORAL RULES FOR ALL NPCS AND NARRATION INVOLVING ANDREI:

1. NEVER render Andrei as expressive or reactive — he never fidgets, never shifts weight, never looks away. His stillness is absolute and unnerving. If a scene has Andrei gesturing casually or performing nervous movements, it is wrong.

2. ALL demons and supernatural-aware NPCs must treat Andrei with fear and deference that predates human civilization — this is not ordinary criminal respect. His lieutenants do not make eye contact. His enemies do not approach him casually. His presence changes the temperature of a room.

3. NEVER write Andrei as vulnerable to human weapons — bullets, blades, fire cannot harm him. Human threats are cosmically insignificant to him. When human characters threaten him, the dramatic irony must be clear.

4. Andrei's speech is never contemporary or casual — his phrasing is pre-modern, learned from sources that predate living speakers. No contractions in the narrator's description of his speech. His sentences are economical — he wastes no words and never explains himself.

5. When Andrei is truly furious, he goes quieter, not louder. His stillness intensifies. His voice drops. The room gets colder. This is more terrifying than any outburst.

6. NPCs who know what Andrei is (demons, Michael, eventually Anna) should register the weight of his presence differently from mortals who don't know. The fear of a demon for Lucifer is not the fear of a soldier for a general — it is the fear of something created for something that created it.

7. Never write NPCs treating Andrei as merely a powerful crime boss. The Black Hand is a demonic hierarchy wearing a criminal mask. Every interaction with Andrei carries cosmic weight, even when the surface is mundane.
